title: Supervisory Stress Test Documentation Credit Risk Models

source.name: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System

summary: The Federal Reserve published documentation detailing the credit risk models intended for use in the 2026 Supervisory Stress Test, covering corporate, commercial real estate, first lien mortgage, home equity, credit card, auto, and other retail loan categories. The document was updated in January 2026 to provide additional transparency on model coefficients and technical corrections.
key_points: Covers seven credit risk model categories: Corporate, CRE, First Lien Mortgage, Home Equity, Credit Card, Auto, and Other Retail
January 2026 revision added transparency on coefficients used in proposed credit risk models
Includes model components, alternatives considered, and key assumptions for each loan category
Documentation is part of DFA Stress Tests 2026 series available on Federal Reserve website
Document is technical in nature, describing estimation sample windows, regression specifications, and spline implementations
why_it_matters: This documentation is relevant to bank supervisors and large financial institutions subject to stress testing requirements. Market participants may use this information to understand how the Fed models credit losses under adverse scenarios, which affects perceived capital adequacy of major banks. However, this is operational/supervisory documentation rather than market-moving content.
market_structure_context: This document pertains to regulatory capital stress testing methodology, not market structure or trading operations. It describes how the Fed models credit risk but does not address market plumbing, trading venues, or operational frameworks.

issues_found: Source is not relevant to market structure or macro catalysts - this is supervisory/regulatory methodology documentation for bank stress testing, not market operations, trading venues, or market-moving information
The document describes technical credit risk model specifications (spline implementations, regression specifications, coefficient estimates) for bank supervisors, not market participants
